title: Salary Negotiation Playbook
description: Comprehensive guide to negotiating your tech compensation package, including strategies, scripts, and common scenarios.
principles: Never give a number first — let them anchor
Always negotiate — companies expect it and build in room
Negotiate the total package, not just base salary
Use competing offers as leverage (but never lie about them)
Be enthusiastic about the role while negotiating — it's not adversarial
Get everything in writing before accepting
The recruiter is not your adversary — they want to close you

components.name: Base Salary
components.notes: Most companies have bands per level. Push to top of band or argue for the next level.

components.name: Signing Bonus
components.notes: Often the most flexible component. Ask for higher bonus if base is at band cap.

components.name: Annual Bonus Target
components.notes: Usually fixed by level. Focus on other components.

components.name: Equity/RSU Grant
components.notes: Significant at senior levels. Ask about vesting schedule, refresh grants, and strike price.

components.name: Start Date
components.notes: Can negotiate for time off between jobs. Some companies backdate RSU grants to earlier start.

components.name: Level
components.notes: The single highest-leverage negotiation. Moving up a level can be worth $50-200K+ in TC.

components.name: Remote Work / Location
components.notes: Some companies adjust pay by location. Negotiate remote flexibility if important.

components.name: PTO / Sabbatical
components.notes: Smaller companies may offer additional PTO. Larger companies have fixed policies.

components.name: Relocation Package
components.notes: If relocating, negotiate for full coverage including temporary housing and moving costs.

scripts.scenario: When asked for salary expectations early in the process
scripts.script: I'm really excited about this opportunity and I'd prefer to learn more about the role and team before discussing compensation. I'm confident we can find a number that works for both of us. Could you share the range budgeted for this position?
scripts.notes: Deflect politely. If pressed, say 'I'm looking for a competitive package in line with market rates for this level.'

scripts.scenario: When you receive the initial offer
scripts.script: Thank you so much for the offer! I'm very excited about joining [Company]. I'd like to take a day or two to review the full package. Could you send me the details in writing?
scripts.notes: Never accept on the spot. Always ask for written details and time to review.

scripts.script: I'm very enthusiastic about this role and the team. After reviewing the offer and considering my experience in [specific area], market data from levels.fyi, and my competing opportunities, I was hoping we could discuss adjusting the base to [target]. I believe this better reflects the value I'll bring, especially given my expertise in [specific skill relevant to the role].
scripts.notes: Cite specific reasons: experience, market data, competing offers, relevant skills.

scripts.script: I want to be transparent — I have another offer from [Company] at [TC number]. I genuinely prefer [their company] because of [specific reasons]. Is there flexibility to make the package more competitive? I want to make the right long-term decision.
scripts.notes: Only mention real offers. Show genuine preference for their company. Let them decide how to match.

scripts.scenario: When they say the offer is firm
scripts.script: I understand the base salary may be at the top of the band. Would there be flexibility on the signing bonus or equity grant? Even a one-time signing bonus of [amount] would help bridge the gap and make this an easy yes.
scripts.notes: If one component is fixed, pivot to others. Signing bonus is often the most flexible.

scripts.script: Based on my [X years] of experience and the scope of work I'd be taking on, I believe [next level] is a better fit. In my current role, I've been operating at that level by [specific examples]. Could we discuss whether [next level] is appropriate?
scripts.notes: This is the highest-leverage negotiation. Have concrete examples of operating at the higher level.

scripts.script: Thank you for working with me on this. I'm thrilled to accept the offer at [confirmed terms]. I'm really looking forward to joining the team on [start date]. Could you send the updated offer letter for my signature?
scripts.notes: Confirm all negotiated terms verbally and get them in the written offer letter before signing.

researchSources.name: Levels.fyi
researchSources.description: Crowdsourced compensation data with level mapping across companies

researchSources.name: Blind
researchSources.description: Anonymous professional network with real compensation discussions

researchSources.name: Comprehensive.io
researchSources.description: Compensation data specifically for tech roles

researchSources.name: H1B Salary Database
researchSources.description: Public H1B salary data filed with the Department of Labor

commonMistakes: Accepting the first offer without negotiating
Giving a number before receiving their offer
Focusing only on base salary and ignoring equity, bonus, and level
Not researching market rates before negotiating
Accepting verbally without getting written confirmation
Burning bridges — you may work with these people someday
